edge: 3D Point Cloud is the input data for 2D Floorplan reconstruction in this context.
Existing approaches typically employ heuristically designed multi-stage pipelines for floorplan reconstruction.
In this context, floorplan reconstruction is formulated as a single-stage structured prediction task to find a variable-size set of polygons.
The novel Transformer architecture generates polygons of multiple rooms in parallel for floorplan reconstruction.
The model features two-level queries for polygons and corners to aid in the reconstruction process.
The floorplan reconstruction model achieves a new state-of-the-art for the Structure3D dataset.
The method achieves significantly faster inference than previous methods for floorplan reconstruction.

edge: The method can be extended to predict additional information such as semantic room types and architectural elements like doors and windows.
The code and models for the method are available at the mentioned URL.
The goal of floorplan reconstruction is to abstract a 3D point cloud into a 2D vector map.
Floorplans are an essential representation that enables a wide range of applications in robotics, AR/VR, interior design, etc.
Several works have shown the effectiveness of projecting the raw 3D point data along the gravity axis to obtain a 2D density map that highlights the building's structural elements.
The resulting density maps are compact and computationally efficient, but inherit the noise and data gaps of the underlying point clouds, hence floorplan reconstruction remains a challenging task.
RoomFormer jointly recovers multiple room polygons along with their associated room types, as well as architectural elements such as doors and windows.
Top-down methods first extract room masks from the density map using neural networks, then employ optimization/search techniques.

Mask R-CNN is an example of neural networks used in the context, while optimization/search techniques like integer programming and Monte-Carlo Tree-Search are employed to extract a polygonal floorplan.
The optimization/search techniques are used to extract a polygonal floorplan in the context.
Bottom-up methods first detect corners as mentioned in the context.
Edges are looked for between corners which represent wall segments according to the context.
RoomFormer is designed to directly map a density image to a set of room polygons as specified in the context.
RoomFormer leverages the sequence prediction capabilities of Transformers to output a variable-length, ordered sequence of vertices per room.
A standard CNN backbone is employed to extract features from the birds-eye view density map as mentioned in the context.
The Transformer architecture employs a standard CNN backbone to extract features from the birds-eye view density map.
The Transformer architecture utilizes a Transformer encoder-decoder setup to process the image features and outputs multiple ordered corner sequences in parallel.

The CNN backbone extracts features from the birds-eye view density map, which are then consumed by the Transformer encoder-decoder setup along with positional encodings to output multiple ordered corner sequences in parallel.
The image features are used to predict ordered corners, which are then connected to recover the floorplan.
The predicted ordered corners are used to recover the floorplan by connecting them in the predicted order.
The corners are part of the room polygons, which need to be connected to form the floorplan.

The room polygons are associated with two-level queries where one level is for the room polygons and the other level is for their corners to address the challenge of varying numbers of rooms and corners.
The queries are refined iteratively through self-attention among them and cross-attention between queries and image features in the decoder.
The polygon matching strategy establishes the correspondence between predictions and targets at both room and corner levels to enable end-to-end training.
The predictions from the model aim to best explain the evidence in the density map by predicting a set of polygons.
Floorplan reconstruction turns raw sensor data (e.g., point clouds, density maps, RGB images) into vectorized geometries.
The RoomFormer model is an end-to-end trainable, Transformer-type architecture that implements the proposed formulation via two-level queries.
The RoomFormer model predicts a set of polygons each consisting of a sequence of vertex coordinates.

Polygon queries are represented as Q ∈ RM×N×2, where M is the maximum number of polygons (i.e., room level) and N is the maximum number of vertices per polygon. Room polygons are mentioned as one level of the two-level polygon queries.
Polygon queries consist of information about vertices with the representation Q ∈ RM×N×2. Vertices are mentioned as part of the two-level polygon queries for representing room structures.

Queries in the decoder consist of content queries (decoder embeddings) and positional queries generated from polygon queries. The decoder layer structure is illustrated in Fig. 3a, showing the relationship between queries and decoder layer.
Content queries in the decoder are represented as embeddings that contribute to the overall query structure. They are part of the decoder query formulation.

Positiional queries in the decoder are generated from polygon queries and play a crucial role in determining the spatial relationships within the room structures. They are key components of the decoder query.

The decoder performs self-attention on all corner-level queries, enabling interaction between corners of a single room. This design allows for reasoning within individual rooms based on corner coordinates.
Corners on the adjacent walls of two rooms can influence each other. This shows the interplay between different room structures and how corners interact across room boundaries.
In the multi-scale deformable attention module, polygon information is directly used to enhance attentive mechanisms. This module utilizes polygon queries for global reasoning within the network.
The multi-scale deformable attention module uses polygon queries as reference points for pooling features from multi-scale feature maps around polygon vertices.
The polygon matching strategy encourages the queries at corner level to follow a specific order while queries at room level can be unordered.
The room polygons can be obtained by connecting valid vertices in a specific order without needing an explicit edge detector.
The vertices in each polygon are refined layer-by-layer in the decoder.
The prediction head (MLP) predicts relative offsets (∆x, ∆y) from decoder embeddings and updates polygon queries for the next layer.
Both decoder embeddings and polygon queries are initialized from a normal distribution and learned as part of the model parameters. They are updated layer-by-layer during inference.
The learned decoder embeddings are used in predicting the final labels after each layer.
The polygon queries help in selecting valid queries and visualizing their position after each layer, contributing to the final predicted labels.
The prediction head of the Transformer decoder outputs M polygons with N vertices, which is a key aspect of the network's prediction process.
Matching the fixed-number predictions with the arbitrary-numbered groundtruth is essential for making the network end-to-end trainable. This matching is done at set and sequence levels.
Each predicted polygon instance consists of valid vertices, represented as 2D coordinates in normalized space [0, 1].
Vertices are part of polygons and contribute to the shape and structure of the polygons.
A polygon can be viewed as a sequence of vertices arranged in a specific order to form a closed shape.
Matching cost is calculated at the sequence level to find the similarity or dissimilarity between groundtruth polygon and predicted polygon.
